axioms.statement: Energy is conserved. Every transformation can be accounted for in joules.
Logically irreversible operations on information have a minimum thermodynamic cost (Landauer's principle).
Flows of energy, materials, and recorded claims can be observed, timestamped, and linked. What is not measured cannot be systematically optimized or held accountable.
A state change is fully accountable only if an inverse exists. Unrecorded irreversible changes destroy auditability.

Long-enduring systems for managing shared resources tend to exhibit a recurring set of design features: clear boundaries, congruent rules, collective choice, monitoring, graduated sanctions, conflict resolution, and nesting (Ostrom).
When the cost of verifying claims is high and the cost of making false or incomplete claims is low, extraction and free-riding become easier to sustain.

Systems can be composed from lower-order units whose interfaces are defined in measurable units. The performance of the whole depends on the integrity of the interfaces and the accounting between units.

postulates.statement: A complete thermodynamic ledger reveals the true energy cost of goods and services, exposing hidden labor extraction.
A reversible, cryptographically linked ledger of joules and actions cannot be quietly rewritten, raising the cost of opacity.
Modular composition of joule-accounted nodes is beneficial only when combined η exceeds individual η, producing fractal scaling without centralization.
Every irreversible computation has a non-zero mass equivalent (E/c²), permanently recordable alongside the joule cost.
Systems that systematically obscure energy and labor flows create conditions where extraction is easier; tools that make those flows visible are concrete forms of resistance to opacity.
